title: The Nervous System and Mindful Creating: How Making Things Heals Trauma
excerpt: Discover how mindful creating engages the nervous system to heal trauma through embodied presence and regulation.
body:
Understanding Trauma Through the Nervous System

Trauma isn’t just a psychological event locked in the past; it’s a physiological experience encoded deeply in the nervous system. As Bessel van der Kolk famously stated, "The body keeps the score"—meaning trauma imprints itself on our bodily systems, often leading to chronic dysregulation, hypervigilance, or numbness.1 When we understand trauma this way, it becomes clear why traditional talk therapy alone sometimes leaves us feeling stuck. Our nervous system holds on to these survival patterns, impacting our emotional and physical wellbeing. Healing, therefore, requires more than cognitive insight—it demands somatic engagement.

Why Creating Matters: The Neuroscience of Making

Engaging in mindful creating—be it painting, knitting, woodworking, or any hands-on craft—activates brain regions linked to focus, sensory integration, and emotional regulation. Peter Levine, a pioneer in somatic trauma therapy, emphasizes that somatic awareness and gentle, intentional movement can help discharge stored trauma.2 Creating something with your hands invites your nervous system into a state of safety and flow, crucial for trauma recovery.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how our autonomic nervous system shifts between states of fight/flight, freeze, and social engagement.3 Mindful making promotes activation of the ventral vagal complex—the part that supports calm, connection, and creative expression. This state allows the body to integrate traumatic memories without being overwhelmed.

Mindful Creating as a Pathway to Nervous System Regulation

Deb Dana, a leading clinician in Polyvagal-informed therapy, advocates using creative, embodied activities to foster nervous system regulation.4 When you immerse yourself in the rhythm of making, your breath, heart rate, and muscle tone often settle into more regulated patterns. This automatic calming effect is the nervous system’s way of telling you it’s safe.

Creating slows down the internal chatter and invites present-moment awareness, which is essential in rewiring trauma responses. Unlike cognitive strategies that can trigger intellectualizing or avoidance, the sensory-rich experience of making grounds you in your body.

The Role of Sensory Engagement and Flow States

Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, highlights the importance of sensory engagement in trauma healing.5 The tactile and visual feedback from creating stimulates interoception—the sense of the internal state of the body—which trauma survivors often struggle to access.6 This sensory input helps re-establish a coherent sense of self.

Flow states, described by psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi, are moments when you are fully absorbed in an activity with a sense of effortless concentration.7 Flow induced by creative making can override trauma-triggered hyperarousal or dissociation, offering a restorative break from survival mode.

How Trauma Impacts Creativity and How to Reclaim It

Gabor Maté discusses how trauma can shut down creativity as a protective mechanism.8 The nervous system prioritizes safety over exploration, so many trauma survivors feel blocked or fearful about self-expression. However, the act of creating mindfully gently challenges these fears, offering a new narrative: that you can express, make, and be safe simultaneously.

This reclaiming process is not about producing art or perfection but about the embodied process of making itself. Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems model aligns here, recognizing parts of ourselves that may resist creating due to trauma but can be engaged with compassion and curiosity.9

Practical Steps to Use Mindful Creating for Trauma Healing

  • Choose a simple, tactile medium: Clay, coloring, knitting, or journaling can be grounding without overwhelming your nervous system.
  • Set an intention: Before you begin, notice your body and breath. Acknowledge any tension or emotion without judgment.
  • Focus on process, not outcome: Let go of expectations. Notice textures, colors, movements.
  • Use breath to anchor: If anxiety arises, pause and breathe deeply to re-establish safety.
  • Allow breaks: Trauma healing is incremental. Respect your limits and return to the activity when ready.

Scientific Support for Creative Practices in Trauma Recovery

Francine Shapiro, developer of EMDR, noted that bilateral stimulation—similar to rhythmic movements in creating—helps process traumatic memories.10 Similarly, art therapy research consistently shows benefits in reducing PTSD symptoms by engaging sensory and emotional parts of the brain.11

Dan Siegel’s work on integration further supports mindful creating, as it fosters coordination between brain hemispheres, nervous system states, and bodily awareness.12 This integration is exactly what helps trauma survivors move from fragmentation to wholeness.

"Mindful creating is more than an art—it is a somatic doorway to safety, presence, and healing." — The Oracle Lover

Conclusion: Making as Medicine for Body and Mind

Trauma recovery is a complex, nonlinear journey. By engaging the nervous system through mindful, embodied creating, you tap into a powerful healing mechanism that complements and extends beyond traditional therapy. The act of making activates safety circuits in the brain, grounds you in the present, and gently reorganizes the nervous system’s trauma imprints.

Remember, this process is about connection—to yourself, your body, and your creative capacity. It’s a practice of patience, curiosity, and compassion. As you make, you heal.

References

  1. van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score. Penguin Books.
  2. Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  3. Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory. Norton & Company.
  4. Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton & Company.
  5. Ogden, P., Minton, K., Pain, C. (2006). Trauma and the Body: A Sensorimotor Approach to Psychotherapy. Norton & Company.
  6. Craig, A. D. (2003). Interoception: The sense of the physiological condition of the body. Current Opinion in Neurobiology, 13(4), 500-505.
  7. Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). Flow: The Psychology of Optimal Experience. Harper & Row.
  8. Maté, G. (2018). The Myth of Normal: Trauma, Illness & Healing in a Toxic Culture. Pan Macmillan.
  9. Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  10. Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy. Guilford Press.
  11. Hass-Cohen, N., Findlay, J. C. (2015). Art Therapy and Clinical Neuroscience. Jessica Kingsley Publishers.
  12. Siegel, D. J. (2012). The Developing Mind. Guilford Press.

faqs.q: How does trauma affect the nervous system?
faqs.a: Trauma causes the nervous system to become dysregulated, often resulting in chronic states of fight, flight, freeze, or shutdown. This impacts emotional and physical health by keeping the body in survival mode.

faqs.q: Why is mindful creating effective for trauma healing?
faqs.a: Mindful creating engages the nervous system in safe, sensory-rich activities that promote regulation and present-moment awareness, helping to discharge stored trauma and rebuild a sense of safety.

faqs.q: Can I use any creative activity for nervous system regulation?
faqs.a: Yes, the key is choosing activities that feel safe and accessible to you, focusing on process over product, and allowing yourself to engage your senses and attention fully.

faqs.q: How do I start mindful creating if I feel blocked or fearful?
faqs.a: Begin with small, low-pressure activities and set gentle intentions. Notice your body's responses and practice self-compassion. Over time, this helps build trust with your creative self.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: A foundational book that explains how trauma affects the body and nervous system, essential for understanding somatic healing.

affiliateProducts.title: In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Offers detailed insight into how somatic awareness and movement can release trauma stored in the body.

affiliateProducts.title: The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
affiliateProducts.description: Provides practical guidance on using Polyvagal-informed approaches, including creative and embodied techniques for nervous system regulation.
